retrieved_docs.title: Bubble CPAP in Infants -- Clinical Significance -- Gaseous Exchange and Work of Breathing
retrieved_docs.content: FRC is increased and maintained by bCPAP, which in turn improves the gas exchange and lowers PaCO 2 . [90] Additionally, the high-frequency oscillatory ventilator-like effect produced by bCPAP may augment the ventilatory effect. [20] Lipsten et al have also noted an improvement in the observed breathing pattern and resistive work of breathing in former preterm infants with mild respiratory distress who received bCPAP therapy. [91] Animal studies have also noted a 40% increase in total lung capacity and a significant increase in lung weight and total protein and DNA content after receiving continuous bCPAP treatment for 2 weeks. [92]

retrieved_docs.title: High-Frequency Oscillator in the Neonate -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Changes in frequency will affect the tidal volume of each delivered breath and in contrast to the amplitude, frequency has an inverse relationship to tidal volume. For example, a lower frequency will increase the tidal volume by increasing the inspiratory time. Therefore, the frequency can be increased to decrease tidal volume and CO 2 elimination in an over-ventilated patient and decreased to increase tidal volume and CO 2 elimination in an under-ventilated patient. Of note, infants with RDS are best managed with a higher frequency (12 to 15 Hz) compared to term infants who need decreased frequencies of 8 to 10 Hz.

retrieved_docs.title: Cord Blood Gas -- Normal and Critical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: Since fetal CO2 gets carried by the umbilical arteries, the expectation is that arterial cord blood gas will be slightly more acidotic than venous cord blood gas. Studies showed that in term infants with uncomplicated delivery, the mean cord arterial pH is 7.24 to 7.27, and the mean cord venous pH 7.32 to 7.34. [13] [14] [15] [16] Preterm newborns were found to have a higher pH, and observations noted a gradual reduction with increasing gestational age. [17] [18] A cord pH less than 7, when combined with other abnormal clinical findings, strongly correlates with adverse neonatal outcomes. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: High-Frequency Oscillator in the Neonate -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Greater than 75% of infants born less than 27 weeks gestation require some form of mechanical ventilation to survive. [1] While survival rates for extremely premature (less than 28 weeks) or very low-birth-weight (VLBW) infants have increased, the incidence of chronic lung disease (CLD) from ventilator-induced lung injury remains high. [2] For this reason, effective lung ventilation and oxygenation are key in preventing CLD.

retrieved_docs.title: Cord Blood Gas -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: In conclusion, cord blood gas analysis is a recommended procedure in all high-risk deliveries. A pH less than 7.00 in non-vigorous newborns strongly correlates with the development of neonatal encephalopathy. Identifying infants at risk is important for the early initiation of neuroprotective therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: Pulmonary Compliance -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Newborn respiratory distress syndrome: Surfactant secretion typically begins between the sixth and seventh months of gestation. Premature newborns may have little or no surfactant in their alveoli at birth, resulting in a significant tendency for their lungs to collapse. As mentioned earlier, surfactant reduces surface tension, thereby increasing lung compliance. The absence of surfactant leads to decreased pulmonary compliance, a condition known as newborn respiratory distress syndrome. This condition can be life-threatening and requires aggressive intervention, including continuous positive pressure breathing.

retrieved_docs.title: Arterial Blood Gas -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Another parameter commonly used in ICUs to assess oxygenation is the oxygenation index (OI). This index is considered a better indicator of lung injury, particularly in the neonatal and pediatric population, compared to the P/F ratio. This index also includes the level of invasive ventilatory support required to maintain oxygenation. [33] The OI is the product of the mean airway pressure (Paw) in cm H 2 O, measured by the ventilator, and the FiO 2 is the percentage divided by the PaO 2 . The OI is commonly used to guide management, such as initiating inhaled nitric oxide, administering surfactant, and defining the potential need for extracorporeal membrane oxygenation. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Persistent Pulmonary Hypertension of the Newborn -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: During normal transition after birth, several events occur simultaneously, which results in a smooth transition to extrauterine life. A drastic fall in pulmonary arterial pressure following the first breath accompanies increased pulmonary blood flow when the umbilical cord is clamped. The increased partial pressure of oxygen and the initiation of ventilation also stimulate the production of vasodilators such as nitric oxide and prostacyclins; cyclic guanosine monophosphate (cGMP) and cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP) mediate pulmonary vasodilation through endothelial nitric oxide. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Bubble CPAP in Infants -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Furthermore, proportional to the degree of prematurity, surfactant production is diminished in preterm neonates, reaching sufficient levels only after 36 weeks gestational age. This leads to neonatal RDS, where increased surface tension in the alveoli due to surfactant deficiency results in reduced lung compliance. This, in turn, causes alveolar collapse, ventilation-perfusion (V-Q) mismatch, decreased functional residual capacity (FRC), and reduced lung volumes, leading to impaired oxygenation and respiratory failure. [13] By addressing the primary issues, bCPAP stimulates/conserves endogenous surfactant production while providing end-distending pressure through positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP) that prevents/reduces alveolar collapse and its downstream effects. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Cord Blood Gas -- Interfering Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Delayed cord clamping may alter blood gas analysis. Sampling should be done soon after birth from the unclamped cord to obtain the most accurate information. Delay in clamping the cord after delivery also decreases the reliability of measurements. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Oxygenation Status and Pulse Oximeter Analysis -- Function -- Definitions Generally Used
retrieved_docs.content: The A-a gradient (or difference) is defined as the alveolar oxygen content minus the arterial oxygen content obtained from an arterial blood gas (ABG). Theoretically, there should be no gradient, but in reality, there is at least a 5 mm Hg or more gradient. The alveolar oxygen content comes from at least 300 to 400 million or more alveoli present at birth and tends to be a calculated value. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Treatment / Management -- Monitoring Oxygenation and Ventilation
retrieved_docs.content: Non-invasive pulse oximetry is now the standard of care to monitor oxygen saturation (SaO2). Unclear higher limits often limit the utility of pulse oximetry, since PaO2 could be significantly higher at the SaO2 levels >95%. Non-invasive capnography and transcutaneous carbon dioxide monitoring are used as adjuncts for monitoring ventilation.

retrieved_docs.title: Bubble CPAP in Infants -- Technique or Treatment -- Weaning and Discontinuation
retrieved_docs.content: But, consensus guidelines for an optimal strategy to wean off bCPAP are lacking. [56] Several weaning methods exist—gradual titration of CPAP (ie, PEEP) and then off respiratory support; gradual transition to other modes of support like nasal cannula (high or low flow); increasing time off CPAP; or sudden withdrawal of CPAP to room air. [57] RCTs and meta-analyses suggest that extremely preterm neonates can be successfully weaned off CPAP by 32 to 33 weeks postmenstrual age and 1600 g of body weight. [54] [55] Similarly, RCTs report preterm neonates, especially those born at less than 28 weeks gestation, have better success when gradually weaned off CPAP when compared to sudden weaning. [55] [58] [59] On the other hand, for a term infant, weaning from CPAP to no respiratory support can also be considered. No matter the strategy, close monitoring of FiO 2 , work of breathing, and airway patency is required while weaning. [60]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1721
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight, early or threatened labor, threatened preterm labor (تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر), short stature (قصر القامة), and neonatal death (وفاة حديث الولادة) are interrelated clinical concepts. Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with short stature, indicating that babies born prematurely with low weight are more likely to develop reduced adult height. Short stature itself is clinically associated with neonatal death, suggesting that children who remain short have an increased risk of dying in the newborn period. Early or threatened labor is used to classify threatened preterm labor, and threatened preterm labor is also clinically associated with short stature. Together, these relationships highlight that low birth weight and threatened preterm labor can contribute to short stature, which in turn is linked to higher neonatal mortality, underscoring the importance of monitoring growth and labor complications in perinatal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2605
retrieved_docs.content: INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature), indicating that infants born preterm with low birth weight often develop reduced linear growth. In turn, قصر القامة is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), suggesting that short stature in this vulnerable population may be linked to an increased risk of death shortly after birth. The Pediatric Adverse Events Terminology encompasses adverse pediatric outcomes, and its subset includes the concepts تدلّي الحبل السري (umbilical cord prolapse) and وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death). Together, these relationships highlight a network of interrelated risk factors and adverse events: preterm low birth weight, short stature, and neonatal death are closely connected, while umbilical cord prolapse is also recognized within the same adverse‑event framework, underscoring the importance of comprehensive monitoring and intervention in high‑risk newborns.

Clinically, it is included within **اضطرابات لدى حديث الولادة (SMQ)**, the neonatal disorders grouping, and is mapped from **حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة** (unspecified condition arising in the perinatal period). This perinatal condition is linked to **عدوى في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة، غير محدد بخلاف ذلك** (nonspecific perinatal infection) and has a broader relationship with **تقييد النمو الجنيني** (fetal growth restriction). **Respiratory distress syndrome** is cross‑referenced with **متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة** (pulmonary immaturity syndrome). Within **اضطرابات لدى حديث الولادة (SMQ)**, additional members such as **ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}** (extreme prematurity of unspecified weight) and **عدوى في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة، غير محدد بخلاف ذلك** reinforce the connection between neonatal respiratory compromise, extreme prematurity, and perinatal infection. retrieved_docs.content: reinforce the connection between neonatal respiratory compromise, extreme prematurity, and perinatal infection. This integrated hierarchy supports comprehensive patient care by aligning gestational age, birth weight, fetal growth, and respiratory complications with their appropriate diagnostic categories across ICD‑10‑AM, WHO perinatal codes, and multilingual clinical terminologies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1610
retrieved_docs.content: INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with الولادة القيصرية (Cesarean delivery) and with قصر القامة (short stature). In addition, تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر (threatened preterm labor) is clinically associated with قصر القامة. These relationships indicate that short stature is linked both to infants born preterm with low birth weight and to the condition of threatened preterm labor, while preterm low‑birth‑weight infants are more likely to be delivered by Cesarean section. Recognizing these interconnections can help clinicians anticipate delivery method needs and monitor growth outcomes in infants at risk for or experiencing preterm birth complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2789
retrieved_docs.content: Other pregnancy and delivery including normal is closely linked with ولادة قبل الأوان, as the two conditions frequently co‑occur. In addition, other pregnancy and delivery including normal has a relationship with الولادة القيصرية, indicating that normal pregnancy and delivery contexts are also associated with Cesarean delivery. الولادة القيصرية is clinically associated with ولادة قبل الأوان, showing that Cesarean delivery often occurs in the setting of preterm birth. Furthermore, INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with الولادة القيصرية, highlighting that infants born preterm with low birth weight are commonly delivered by Cesarean section. Together, these interconnections underscore the overlapping nature of normal pregnancy and delivery categories, preterm birth, Cesarean delivery, and low‑birth‑weight infants in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2825
retrieved_docs.content: INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature), indicating that infants born preterm with low birth weight often experience growth restriction that results in reduced adult height. In turn, قصر القامة is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), suggesting that short stature in this context reflects severe underlying health challenges that increase the risk of death shortly after birth. Recognizing the link between low‑birth‑weight preterm infants, subsequent short stature, and heightened neonatal mortality can guide clinicians in early monitoring and intervention strategies to improve outcomes for this vulnerable population.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1449
retrieved_docs.content: Preterm low‑birth‑weight infants are clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature). Short stature itself is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), indicating that children who remain short may have an increased risk of dying in the newborn period. Additionally, تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر (threatened preterm labor) is clinically associated with قصر القامة, suggesting that pregnancies complicated by early labor threats often result in offspring who develop short stature. Collectively, these relationships highlight short stature as a common clinical link among preterm low birth weight, threatened preterm labor, and adverse neonatal outcomes such as neonatal death, underscoring its relevance for risk assessment and early intervention in perinatal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2763
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation constitute a severe perinatal condition that is classified as an extremely premature birth of the highest degree with unspecified weight (ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}). This classification highlights that the infant is born far before term, exhibits markedly reduced body weight, and shows evidence of impaired intra‑uterine growth, all of which place the newborn at high risk for morbidity and mortality and necessitate intensive neonatal monitoring and intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 943
retrieved_docs.content: Certain conditions originating in the perinatal period (P00‑P96) are situated within the broader category of Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation, which is classified as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}. Within the perinatal‑originating conditions, there is a more specific subgroup of Respiratory and cardiovascular disorders specific to the perinatal period (P19‑P29). This hierarchical organization indicates that infants born with extreme prematurity and unspecified low birth weight are at risk for a range of perinatal conditions, including distinct respiratory and cardiovascular disorders, highlighting the importance of recognizing the underlying gestational and growth‑related factors when assessing and managing newborn health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2320
retrieved_docs.content: The gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is directly linked to the outcome of a liveborn infant, indicating that reaching this stage of pregnancy can result in a newborn who is alive at birth. In the broader classification of pregnancy timelines, the concept of Duration of gestation encompasses liveborn as a specific category or outcome, reflecting that livebirth is considered one of the possible results within the overall spectrum of gestational durations. This relationship underscores the clinical relevance of gestational age in determining the likelihood of a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2842
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with short stature (قصر القامة), and short stature (قصر القامة) is clinically associated with a threat of premature labor (تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر). This interrelationship indicates that infants born preterm with low birth weight are more likely to develop reduced height, and that reduced height itself may signal an increased risk of threatened premature labor. Recognizing these connections can help clinicians monitor growth patterns and anticipate obstetric complications, thereby improving patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2902
retrieved_docs.content: Certain conditions originating in the perinatal period (P00‑P96) represent a broad category of early‑life health problems, and within this group the respiratory and cardiovascular disorders specific to the perinatal period (P19‑P29) are classified as a child subgroup. This hierarchical relationship indicates that the latter are a subset of the broader perinatal condition category, emphasizing the need to consider these specific respiratory and cardiovascular issues when assessing and managing perinatal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1723
retrieved_docs.content: The term خديج designates infants who have completed 29–30 weeks of gestation, and this developmental stage is directly linked to the occurrence of متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة (pulmonary immaturity syndrome). Consequently, babies classified as خديج are at risk for respiratory complications arising from lung immaturity, highlighting the importance of recognizing this gestational age category when assessing and managing neonatal respiratory health.
